_cite.reason: item_type correction: route item to its true surface by generated-brief substance (Technology folded per operator Option B 2026-06-07)

prior.title: Clark County Environment and Sustainability Department - Air Quality and Dust Control Services
prior.summary: Clark County's Environment and Sustainability Department provides air quality management services including dust control permitting, dust monitoring classes, air quality complaint processing, and compliance enforcement. The department offers online portals for permit applications, forms, and dust class registrations.
prior.full_brief:
# Clark County Environment and Sustainability Department — Air Quality and Dust Control Services

## Operations Profile

---

# Purpose and Scope of This Document

This brief profiles the air quality permitting and dust control regulatory environment administered by the Clark County Department of Environment and Sustainability (DES), Division of Air Quality (DAQ), as it applies to freight, logistics, and construction-adjacent operations in Clark County, Nevada. The workspace profile drives all analysis: operations involving ground transport, drayage, road fleet activity, construction site logistics, and any stationary source activity within Clark County are the primary focus.

The brief synthesises seven source blocks. All FACT claims are span-grounded to a verbatim substring of a provided source. ANALYSIS claims are prefixed with the required label token. Items requiring legal interpretation are flagged *Legal Confirmation Required:*.

Convention notes: "the workspace" refers to the freight forwarding and logistics operation described in the workspace profile. "DAQ" refers to the Clark County Division of Air Quality throughout.

---

# What This Regulatory Environment Is and Why It Applies to the Workspace

**What it is.** The Clark County DES Division of Air Quality is the local air pollution control authority for Clark County, Nevada. FACT: "By authority of NRS 445B.500, the Clark County Board of County Commissioners established the Department of Air Quality Management in 2001." *Source: Clark County DES – Division of Air Quality, Clark County NV, 2026. https://www.clarkcountynv.gov/government/departments/environment_and_sustainability/division_of_air_quality/

FACT: "Air Quality administers the air pollution control program for Clark County under provisions of the Clark County Air Quality Regulations." *Source: Clark County DES – Division of Air Quality, Clark County NV, 2026. https://www.clarkcountynv.gov/government/departments/environment_and_sustainability/division_of_air_quality/

FACT: "The mission of the department is to develop and implement high-quality, effective local programs to fulfill air quality regulatory requirements and address community concerns, thereby protecting the region's quality of life while facilitating orderly growth." *Source: Clark County DES – Division of Air Quality, Clark County NV, 2026. https://www.clarkcountynv.gov/government/departments/environment_and_sustainability/division_of_air_quality/

**Federal integration.** FACT: The EPA page for Clark County confirms the county's Air Quality Regulations form part of the Nevada State Implementation Plan (SIP), and the EPA Pacific Southwest Region (Region 9) "implements and enforces federal environmental laws in Arizona, California, Hawaii, Nevada, the Pacific Islands, and 148 Tribal Nations." *Source: U.S. EPA – EPA-Approved Air Quality Regulations for Clark County, U.S. EPA, July 2023. https://www.epa.gov/air-quality-implementation-plans/epa-approved-air-quality-regulations-clark-county

**Why it applies to the workspace.** Operational implication: Any freight or logistics operation in Clark County that conducts soil-disturbing activities (loading yards, temporary commercial staging areas, construction-related logistics), operates stationary emission sources, or manages vehicle fleets touching Clark County roads falls within DAQ's jurisdictional reach under the Clark County Air Quality Regulations. This is especially relevant for road drayage, port/terminal transfers to truck, and any ground-side logistics infrastructure in the Las Vegas metro area.

---

# Issues Requiring Immediate Action

**MONITORING — Dust Control Operating Permit (DCOP) threshold check for any active Clark County ground operations**

Workspaces with road-primary or drayage operations who manage or commission any ground disturbance in Clark County should verify whether current activities meet DCOP trigger thresholds. FACT: "A Dust Control Operating Permit is required for soil disturbing or construction activities 0.25 acres or greater in overall area, mechanized trenching one hundred (100) feet or greater in length, or for mechanical demolition of any structure one thousand (1,000) square feet or larger, or for Temporary Commercial Activities 0.25 acres or greater in overall area." *Source: Clark County DES – Dust Control Permitting Portal, Forms & Requirements, Clark County NV, 2026. https://www.clarkcountynv.gov/government/departments/environment_and_sustainability/division_of_air_quality/permitting/applications_forms/dust-permitting-forms

Analytical inference: Temporary event staging, outdoor freight marshalling areas, and large-scale equipment laydown yards are candidate "Temporary Commercial Activities" under the 0.25-acre threshold — a footprint equivalent to roughly 10,890 square feet. Workspaces operating in live events, film/TV, or humanitarian logistics with outdoor ground use in Clark County should assess acreage before each deployment.

Action: Measure total disturbed area for any active or planned Clark County ground operation against the 0.25-acre threshold before commencing. If at or above threshold, initiate DCOP application via the online portal prior to ground disturbance.

Contact for permit questions: FACT: "please contact an Air Quality Specialist at the Department of Environment and Sustainability, Division of Air Quality's (DAQ) office at 702-455-1524 or by email at aqdcp@clarkcountynv.gov." *Source: Clark County DES – Dust Control Permitting Portal, Forms & Requirements, Clark County NV, 2026. https://www.clarkcountynv.gov/government/departments/environment_and_sustainability/division_of_air_quality/permitting/applications_forms/dust-permitting-forms

---

**MONITORING — Stationary source permit applicability check for any Clark County facility**

Workspaces operating or planning to operate a facility in Clark County (warehouse, depot, generator, fuel handling) should confirm whether the facility qualifies as a minor or major stationary source under DAQ regulations. FACT: "DAQ issues major and minor stationary source permits (AQRs 12.0–12.5 and 12.11) as well as dust control operating permits (AQR 94)." *Source: UNR Business Environmental Program – Clark County Air Quality Assistance, UNRBEP, 2026. https://unrbep.org/services/air-quality-assistance-homepage/clark-county-air-assistance/

FACT: "The minor source permitting thresholds for the regulated pollutants can be found in AQR 12.1.1(e) and the significant thresholds for triggering a controls analysis (RACT) can be found in AQR 12.1.1(k)." *Source: UNR Business Environmental Program – Clark County Air Quality Assistance, UNRBEP, 2026. https://unrbep.org/services/air-quality-assistance-homepage/clark-county-air-assistance/

Action: For any Clark County facility, confirm emission levels against AQR 12.1.1(e) thresholds. *Legal Confirmation Required:* Whether a freight depot or consolidation warehouse with backup generators and fuel storage crosses the minor-source threshold under AQR 12.1.1(e) requires site-specific review by counsel or a qualified environmental consultant.

---

**MONITORING — Recent rule amendments effective 2025–2026**

Two dust control sections were recently amended. FACT: Section 92 (Fugitive Dust Control Requirements for Unpaved Parking Lots and Storage Areas) was "Amended 12/17/2024 – Effective 1/1/2025 (Ordinance 5205)." *Source: Clark County DES – Current Air Quality Regulations, Clark County NV, amended 2/4/2025. https://www.clarkcountynv.gov/government/departments/environment_and_sustainability/division_of_air_quality/rules___regulations/current-aq-rules

FACT: Section 94 (Permitting and Dust Control for Construction and Temporary Commercial Activities) was "Amended 12/17/2024 – Effective 1/1/2025 (Ordinance 5205)." *Source: Clark County DES – Current Air Quality Regulations, Clark County NV, amended 2/4/2025. https://www.clarkcountynv.gov/government/departments/environment_and_sustainability/division_of_air_quality/rules___regulations/current-aq-rules

FACT: Section 12.0 (Applicability and General Requirements for Permitting Stationary Sources) was "Amended 3/17/2026 – Effective 4/1/2026 (Ordinance 5353)." *Source: Clark County DES – Current Air Quality Regulations, Clark County NV, amended 2/4/2025. https://www.clarkcountynv.gov/government/departments/environment_and_sustainability/division_of_air_quality/rules___regulations/current-aq-rules

Operational implication: Any Clark County dust permit compliance plan or stationary source permit application filed before January 2025 or before April 2026 may not reflect the current rule text. Re-confirmation against the current AQR versions is warranted.

Re-check window: Monitor DAQ's "Proposed Revisions / Notices & Workshops / Recently Adopted" page quarterly for any further Section 92, 94, or 12.x amendments.

---

# How the Workspace Sits in the Compliance Chain

The workspace's supply-chain role in Clark County operations determines which DAQ obligations attach.

**As a freight forwarder / logistics operator managing ground-side operations:**
Operational implication: Where the workspace directs or commissions ground disturbance (staging areas, temporary yards, demolition for warehouse build-outs) exceeding the regulatory thresholds in AQR 94.4.1(b), the DCOP obligation is triggered by the activity itself regardless of whether the workspace is the property owner.

*Legal Confirmation Required:* Whether the workspace — acting as logistics operator rather than property owner or general contractor — is the obligated permit applicant under AQR 94 for a Temporary Commercial Activity, or whether that obligation falls on the property owner or a contracted construction manager, requires counsel review of AQR 94 text and applicable Nevada law.

**As a road fleet operator:**
FACT: Section 45 (Idling of Diesel Powered Motor Vehicles) is an active regulation, "Amended 8/3/2021 (Ordinance 4878)." *Source: Clark County DES – Current Air Quality Regulations, Clark County NV, amended 2/4/2025. https://www.clarkcountynv.gov/government/departments/environment_and_sustainability/division_of_air_quality/rules___regulations/current-aq-rules

*Specific penalty/enforcement thresholds under Section 45 not available from primary sources as of 2026-05-28. Legal Confirmation Required: review Section 45 text directly from the DAQ AQR index for idle-time limits and driver/operator obligations applicable to diesel drayage vehicles operating within Clark County.*

**As a stationary-source facility operator (warehouse, depot, fuel handling):**
Analytical inference: The general permit pathway (AQR 12.11) is likely the most relevant entry point for facilities that fall below major-source thresholds; the authority-to-operate pathway under AQR 12.4 applies to Part 70 (Title V) sources, which are large industrial emitters unlikely to apply to standard freight facilities.

FACT: "DAQ issues an authority to operate under the general permit program for most gasoline dispensing facilities and soil and groundwater remediation operations." *Source: UNR Business Environmental Program – Clark County Air Quality Assistance, UNRBEP, 2026. https://unrbep.org/services/air-quality-assistance-homepage/clark-county-air-assistance/

---

# Substantive Requirements

## Dust Control Operating Permit (DCOP) — AQR Section 94

**Trigger thresholds (in force as of 1 January 2025, Ordinance 5205):**

FACT: The DCOP portal states the permit is required for "soil disturbing or construction activities 0.25 acres or greater in overall area, mechanized trenching one hundred (100) feet or greater in length, or for mechanical demolition of any structure one thousand (1,000) square feet or larger, or for Temporary Commercial Activities 0.25 acres or greater in overall area." *Source: Clark County DES – Dust Control Permitting Portal, Forms & Requirements, Clark County NV, 2026. https://www.clarkcountynv.gov/government/departments/environment_and_sustainability/division_of_air_quality/permitting/applications_forms/dust-permitting-forms

FACT: "The thresholds for requiring a dust control operating permit for construction or temporary commercial activities can be found in AQR 94.4.1(b)." *Source: UNR Business Environmental Program – Clark County Air Quality Assistance, UNRBEP, 2026. https://unrbep.org/services/air-quality-assistance-homepage/clark-county-air-assistance/

**Application process:**

FACT: Permit applications are submitted online through the Dust Control Permitting Portal, which is "not compatible with the Safari browser." *Source: Clark County DES – Dust Control Permitting Portal, Forms & Requirements, Clark County NV, 2026. https://www.clarkcountynv.gov/government/departments/environment_and_sustainability/division_of_air_quality/permitting/applications_forms/dust-permitting-forms

Operational implication: The online portal is the primary submission channel; field teams relying on mobile Safari browsers cannot submit applications directly — an alternative device or browser is required.

**Pre-application area verification:**

FACT: "Prior to submitting a DCOP application, please review the area on the AQ Map Assistant to verify that it is not already permitted." *Source: Clark County DES – Dust Control Permitting Portal, Forms & Requirements, Clark County NV, 2026. https://www.clarkcountynv.gov/government/departments/environment_and_sustainability/division_of_air_quality/permitting/applications_forms/dust-permitting-forms

**Supplemental plan requirement for large sites:**

FACT: The portal references "Instructions for Completing the Dust Mitigation Plan Supplement for Projects 10 Acres or Larger," indicating a separate Dust Mitigation Plan Supplement is required for projects at or above 10 acres. *Source: Clark County DES – Dust Control Permitting Portal, Forms & Requirements, Clark County NV, 2026. https://www.clarkcountynv.gov/government/departments/environment_and_sustainability/division_of_air_quality/permitting/applications_forms/dust-permitting-forms

**On-site signage:**

FACT: The permitting page lists "Dust Control Permit Sign – Design and Posting of Signage Guidance" as a required compliance document, with recommendations for "minimum sign sizes, posting guidance and various sign examples." *Source: Clark County DES – Dust Control Permitting Portal, Forms & Requirements, Clark County NV, 2026. https://www.clarkcountynv.gov/government/departments/environment_and_sustainability/division_of_air_quality/permitting/applications_forms/dust-permitting-forms

**Dust monitoring on site:**

FACT: The portal lists the "Construction Site Dust Control Monitor Form" and the "Record of Daily Dust Control for Construction Activities – Dust Monitor Use" as available forms, indicating on-site monitoring and daily recordkeeping are part of the compliance framework. *Source: Clark County DES – Dust Control Permitting Portal, Forms & Requirements, Clark County NV, 2026. https://www.clarkcountynv.gov/government/departments/environment_and_sustainability/division_of_air_quality/permitting/applications_forms/dust-permitting-forms

**Dust monitor class requirement:**

FACT: The portal directs users: "To enroll in a dust monitor class, please visit our Dust Monitor Classes webpage for instructions." *Source: Clark County DES – Dust Control Permitting Portal, Forms & Requirements, Clark County NV, 2026. https://www.clarkcountynv.gov/government/departments/environment_and_sustainability/division_of_air_quality/permitting/applications_forms/dust-permitting-forms

Analytical inference: For workspaces that deploy on-site supervisors or project managers to Clark County ground operations, ensuring at least one responsible party has completed the DAQ Dust Monitor Class is a practical compliance prerequisite before applying for a DCOP.

## Fugitive Dust — AQR Section 92 (Unpaved Parking Lots and Storage Areas)

FACT: Section 92 is titled "Fugitive Dust Control Requirements for Unpaved Parking Lots and Storage Areas" and was most recently "Amended 12/17/2024 – Effective 1/1/2025 (Ordinance 5205)." *Source: Clark County DES – Current Air Quality Regulations, Clark County NV, amended 2/4/2025. https://www.clarkcountynv.gov/government/departments/environment_and_sustainability/division_of_air_quality/rules___regulations/current-aq-rules

FACT: "One of the control measures that can be used to meet the stabilization standards for new unpaved storage areas is the application of Alternative Asphalt Paving. This option must be approved by DAQ in writing before it is implemented." *Source: Clark County DES – Dust Control Permitting Portal, Forms & Requirements, Clark County NV, 2026. https://www.clarkcountynv.gov/government/departments/environment_and_sustainability/division_of_air_quality/permitting/applications_forms/dust-permitting-forms

Operational implication: Workspaces operating temporary freight marshalling or equipment staging on unpaved surfaces in Clark County fall under Section 92 stabilisation standards. Any plan to use alternative paving as a dust control method requires prior written DAQ approval — this is a lead-time item that should be built into site preparation schedules.

## General Fugitive Dust — AQR Section 41

FACT: Section 41 (Fugitive Dust) was most recently "Amended 1/21/2020" and EPA approval was confirmed effective 5/19/2022 ("Section 41 Fugitive Dust 01/21/2020 87 FR 30423, 5/19/2022"). *Source: U.S. EPA – EPA-Approved Air Quality Regulations for Clark County, U.S. EPA, July 2023. https://www.epa.gov/air-quality-implementation-plans/epa-approved-air-quality-regulations-clark-county

*Specific threshold figures and penalty provisions for Section 41 not available from primary sources as of 2026-05-28. Obtain the current Section 41 text from the DAQ AQR index at https://www.clarkcountynv.gov/government/departments/environment_and_sustainability/division_of_air_quality/rules___regulations/current-aq-rules for site-specific analysis.*

## Stationary Source Permitting — AQR Sections 12.0–12.11

FACT: Sections 12.2, 12.3, 12.4, and 12.5 were "Amended 5/19/2026 (Ordinance 5375) – Effective 6/3/2026." *Source: Clark County DES – Current Air Quality Regulations, Clark County NV, amended 2/4/2025. https://www.clarkcountynv.gov/government/departments/environment_and_sustainability/division_of_air_quality/rules___regulations/current-aq-rules

Analytical inference: For any workspace facility permitted before June 2026, a review of the amended Sections 12.2–12.5 is warranted to confirm continued compliance with updated applicability, PSD, nonattainment, and Title V requirements post-6 June 2026.

## Annual Emissions Inventory Reporting — AQR Section 12.9

FACT: Section 12.9 (Annual Emissions Statement and Inventory Requirement) was "Amended 8/4/2020 – Effective 8/18/2020," with EPA SIP approval for Section 12.9.1 confirmed "8/18/2020 87 FR 45657, 7/29/2022." *Source: U.S. EPA – EPA-Approved Air Quality Regulations for Clark County, U.S. EPA, July 2023. https://www.epa.gov/air-quality-implementation-plans/epa-approved-air-quality-regulations-clark-county

*Specific annual emissions reporting deadlines and filing formats under Section 12.9 not available from primary sources as of 2026-05-28. Confirm current submission schedule directly with DAQ.*

## Visible Air Contaminants — AQR Section 26

FACT: Section 26 (Emission of Visible Air Contaminants) was approved into the Nevada SIP: "Section 26 Emission of Visible Air Contaminants 5/5/2016 82 FR 27622, 6/16/2017." *Source: U.S. EPA – EPA-Approved Air Quality Regulations for Clark County, U.S. EPA, July 2023. https://www.epa.gov/air-quality-implementation-plans/epa-approved-air-quality-regulations-clark-county

Operational implication: Road fleet operators in Clark County with diesel equipment should confirm vehicles meet Section 26 visible emissions standards; enforcement notices are issued by DAQ's Compliance and Enforcement Section.

---

# Registration and Reporting Obligations

**Stationary source permitting:** FACT: "Stationary source air permits, including Title V permits, are issued through the Permitting Section." *Source: Clark County DES – Division of Air Quality, Clark County NV, 2026. https://www.clarkcountynv.gov/government/departments/environment_and_sustainability/division_of_air_quality/

**Compliance / Annual Emissions Inventory Reporting Forms:** FACT: The DAQ portal lists "Compliance/Annual Emissions Inventory Reporting Forms" as a distinct application form category available for download. *Source: Clark County DES – Division of Air Quality, Clark County NV, 2026. https://www.clarkcountynv.gov/government/departments/environment_and_sustainability/division_of_air_quality/

**Online document submission shift:** FACT: The DAQ page notes "Air Quality Permitting, Compliance Documents to Require Hard-Copy Submission Only," indicating a recent policy change away from (or returning to) hard-copy submission for some document types. *Source: Clark County DES – Division of Air Quality, Clark County NV, 2026. https://www.clarkcountynv.gov/government/departments/environment_and_sustainability/division_of_air_quality/

Analytical inference: The hard-copy submission notice suggests a procedural change that could affect permit application timelines; workspaces should confirm with DAQ whether hard-copy submission is now the default for their specific permit type before investing time in digital-only submissions.

**Dust class registration:** FACT: The DES homepage lists "Dust classes" and "Dust Monitor Classes" as registrable activities, with an "Online Dust Class and Dust Monitor Class Registration Process" accessible via the DAQ portal. *Source: Clark County DES – Dust Control Permitting Portal, Forms & Requirements, Clark County NV, 2026. https://www.clarkcountynv.gov/government/departments/environment_and_sustainability/division_of_air_quality/permitting/applications_forms/dust-permitting-forms

**State-level interaction:** FACT: The Nevada DEP (NDEP) air permitting guidance page is a separate state-level resource that directs Clark County facilities to DES/DAQ as the jurisdictional air district. *Source: Nevada DEP (NDEP) – Air Quality Permit Guidance, NDEP, 2026. https://ndep.nv.gov/air/permitting/permit-guidance

Analytical inference: For multi-jurisdictional operations that cross Clark County and other Nevada counties, the workspace will encounter NDEP as the permitting authority for facilities outside Clark County, while DAQ retains authority within Clark County — dual-track permit management is the correct operating model.

---

# Operational System Requirements

## 1. DCOP Pre-Application Area Measurement and Verification

**Scope:** Any Clark County ground operation at or approaching the 0.25-acre or 100-linear-foot trigger.
**Deadline:** Before ground disturbance commences.
**Gap from current state:** Workspaces without a standing procedure to measure and document site footprints before each Clark County deployment lack a key compliance trigger gate. The AQ Map Assistant (available via the DAQ portal) provides the tool for pre-application verification.

## 2. Dust Monitor Training — Personnel Certification

**Scope:** On-site supervisors for Clark County ground operations.
**Deadline:** Before DCOP activity commences; DAQ requires on-site dust monitoring as a permit condition.
**Gap from current state:** Workspaces that rotate project managers across sites should maintain a registry of Dust Monitor Class-certified personnel and confirm availability before deploying to Clark County.
**Contact:** FACT: Dust Monitor Class registration is conducted via the DAQ online registration process referenced at https://www.clarkcountynv.gov/government/departments/environment_and_sustainability/division_of_air_quality/permitting/applications_forms/dust-permitting-forms.

## 3. Permit Tracking and Record-Keeping

**Scope:** Active DCOPs, stationary source permits, compliance documents.
**Deadline:** Ongoing; permit conditions run with the activity.
**Gap from current state:** FACT: "A list of Active Dust Control Permits by Area can be found on our Records Request webpage in the form of a searchable pdf." *Source: Clark County DES – Dust Control Permitting Portal, Forms & Requirements, Clark County NV, 2026. https://www.clarkcountynv.gov/government/departments/environment_and_sustainability/division_of_air_quality/permitting/applications_forms/dust-permitting-forms — workspaces should cross-reference this searchable PDF before commencing work on any parcel to avoid double-permitting conflicts.

## 4. Rule Amendment Monitoring — Sections 12.0–12.5 (Effective 6 June 2026)

**Scope:** Any facility with an existing stationary source permit, or any workspace planning a new Clark County facility in 2026.
**Deadline:** Confirm compliance with amended Sections 12.2, 12.3, 12.4, 12.5 before or immediately after 3 June 2026 effective date.
**Gap from current state:** Workspaces that have not reviewed the Ordinance 5375 amendments to the major-source permitting sections face potential compliance gap from 3 June 2026 onward.

---

# Small Business Assistance — No-Cost Compliance Resource

FACT: "The Clark County Small Business Assistance Program provides no-cost permitting and compliance assistance for facilities in Clark County. If you're a small business (100 or fewer employees) and not a major stationary source for air pollutants, you qualify for their services. Contact them by calling 702-455-1660 or sending an email to AQSBAP@ClarkCountyNV.gov." *Source: UNR Business Environmental Program – Clark County Air Quality Assistance, UNRBEP, 2026. https://unrbep.org/services/air-quality-assistance-homepage/clark-county-air-assistance/

Analytical inference: For smaller freight forwarding operations or sub-contractors operating in Clark County, the SBAP represents a no-cost pre-application advisory resource that can reduce the risk of filing incomplete or incorrect permit applications. Engaging SBAP before a first DCOP or minor-source permit submission is a low-cost risk-reduction step.

---

# Confirmed Regulatory Timeline

| Date | Event | Status |
|---|---|---|
| 1 January 2019 | AQR 12.1 (Minor Source Permit Requirements) effective | In force |
| 1 January 2019 | AQR 12.11 (General Permits for Minor Stationary Sources) effective | In force |
| 8/18/2020 | AQR 12.9.1 (Annual Emissions Statement) effective | In force |
| 8/3/2021 | Section 45 (Diesel Idling) amended via Ordinance 4878 | In force |
| 5/19/2022 | Section 41 (Fugitive Dust) EPA SIP approval effective | In force |
| 6/14/2023 | Sections 12.0, 12.1, 12.11 EPA SIP approval effective | In force |
| 1 January 2025 | Sections 92 and 94 amended via Ordinance 5205 — current dust control rule text | In force |
| 4 February 2025 | AQR Table of Contents updated | In force |
| 4 February 2025 | Section 0 (Definitions) amended via Ordinance 4878, effective 3/5/2024 | In force |
| 1 April 2026 | Section 12.0 (Applicability and General Requirements for Permitting Stationary Sources) amended via Ordinance 5353 | In force as of 4/1/2026 |
| 3 June 2026 | Sections 12.2, 12.3, 12.4, 12.5 amended via Ordinance 5375 | Effective 6/3/2026 — MONITORING |

*Source for all timeline entries: Clark County DES – Current Air Quality Regulations, Clark County NV, amended 2/4/2025. https://www.clarkcountynv.gov/government/departments/environment_and_sustainability/division_of_air_quality/rules___regulations/current-aq-rules; U.S. EPA – EPA-Approved Air Quality Regulations for Clark County, U.S. EPA, July 2023. https://www.epa.gov/air-quality-implementation-plans/epa-approved-air-quality-regulations-clark-county

**Penalty provisions:** *Specific civil penalty figures under Clark County AQR Section 09 (Civil Penalties) not available from primary sources as of 2026-05-28. The section is titled "Civil Penalties – Amended 12/17/2019" and is listed in the AQR index; obtain the current Section 09 text from the DAQ AQR portal for enforcement exposure analysis.*

**Re-check window:** Pull the DAQ AQR index and "Proposed Revisions / Recently Adopted" page no later than September 2026 to capture any post-Ordinance 5375 rulemaking and to confirm Section 12.5 EPA SIP review outcome (currently noted as "under EPA review").

---

## New Sources Identified

| Source Name | URL | Tier estimate (1–7) | Why this source matters |
|---|---|---|---|
| Clark County DES – Division of Air Quality (Official Department Page) | https://www.clarkcountynv.gov/government/departments/environment_and_sustainability/division_of_air_quality/ | 1 | Primary official DAQ page confirming NRS 445B.500 authority, permit sections issued, and mission; essential for any Clark County AQ permitting analysis. |
| Clark County DES – Dust Control Permitting Portal, Forms & Requirements | https://www.clarkcountynv.gov/government/departments/environment_and_sustainability/division_of_air_quality/permitting/applications_forms/dust-permitting-forms | 1 | Deep official page providing verbatim DCOP threshold language, portal instructions, and supplemental form requirements; cannot be substituted with the general DES homepage. |
| Clark County DES – Current Air Quality Regulations (Full Rule Index) | https://www.clarkcountynv.gov/government/departments/environment_and_sustainability/division_of_air_quality/rules___regulations/current-aq-rules | 1 | Authoritative regulation index with amendment dates and ordinance numbers for all active AQR sections; required for timeline accuracy and amendment tracking. |
| U.S. EPA – EPA-Approved Air Quality Regulations for Clark County (Nevada SIP) | https://www.epa.gov/air-quality-implementation-plans/epa-approved-air-quality-regulations-clark-county | 1 | Federal confirmation of SIP status for Clark County AQRs; provides EPA approval dates and Federal Register citations needed to establish regulatory standing at the federal level. |
| Nevada DEP (NDEP) – Air Quality Permit Guidance | https://ndep.nv.gov/air/permitting/permit-guidance | 2 | State-level regulator page confirming DAQ as the jurisdictional authority for Clark County; essential for multi-county Nevada operations to understand dual-track permitting. |
